summary: Muratori introduces Prime Pussify, a Photoshop script he created for the comic Meow the Infinite that automatically jitters font attributes on a per-character basis. The script solves the problem that Photoshop's built-in text tools are too limited to easily randomize character properties like baseline, size, and spacing. He argues that adding jitter programmatically is superior to using fonts with built-in jitter, since those tend to look repetitive with identical letter sequences. The post serves as a practical tool announcement with usage instructions and a note about Photoshop's scripting limitations.
key_points: Photoshop's text effect tools are too limited to efficiently jitter individual character attributes, requiring manual per-character adjustments
Fonts with built-in jitter look repetitive because identical letter sequences produce identical appearances, making programmatic randomization superior
The script randomizes baseline, size, and spacing per character, and can be re-run repeatedly to find a pleasing randomization
Photoshop's scripting API is severely limited — scripts cannot create real UI elements, only basic text input dialogs
money_quotes: Fonts that are jittery by design often look too repetitive, since the same letter sequences will tend to look the same, so it's usually better to add jitter than to build it into the font anyway.
But Photoshop's text tools are extremely limited, and don't allow you to quickly accomplish an effect like this.
To really jitter a line of text, you'd have to manually select each letter and change the font properties one by one.
As far as I can tell, Photoshop does not allow scripts to pop up any actual UI.
key_insight: When creative tools lack the automation you need, writing a simple script to randomize per-character attributes produces better results than relying on pre-built jittery fonts or tedious manual adjustments.
